column1: What is the Initial Access tactic (TA0001)?
column2: Techniques used to gain a first foothold within a target network — including exploiting public-facing services, supply-chain compromise, and social engineering attacks like phishing

column1: What is phishing in the context of initial access?
column2: When an adversary communicates directly with victims, typically via email, to trick them into carrying out an action or revealing sensitive information

column1: What is the phishing taxonomy proposed by Mariusz Banach?
column2: DELIVERY(CONTAINER(TRIGGER + PAYLOAD + DECOY)) — delivery is how it reaches the victim, container packages the files, trigger executes the payload, payload is the malicious code, and decoy is what the victim sees afterward

column1: What is the purpose of a decoy in an initial access payload?
column2: To display legitimate-looking content to the victim after they interact with the trigger, continuing the social engineering pretext — for example showing a PDF if the trigger was disguised as a PDF

column1: What is the purpose of using complex, multi-stage infection chains?
column2: To make it harder for security solutions to detect the campaign automatically and for analysts to understand what is happening during investigation

column1: What is DLL side-loading?
column2: A technique where an adversary drops a malicious DLL with the correct filename into a writeable location in the DLL search order, causing a legitimate application to load the malicious DLL when it runs

column1: What is the Windows Component Store (WinSxS) and why is it relevant to DLL side-loading?
column2: A directory at C:\Windows\WinSxS that stores multiple versions of the same dependencies — older, vulnerable versions of applications may exist here even after the main OS has been patched, providing candidates for DLL hijacking

column1: What is an AppDomainManager hijack?
column2: A technique that forces a .NET Framework application to load a custom DLL by creating a class that inherits from AppDomainManager — the malicious code runs when the application loads

column1: What are the two methods to make a .NET application load a custom AppDomainManager DLL?
column2: Via environment variables APPDOMAIN_MANAGER_ASM and APPDOMAIN_MANAGER_TYPE, or via appDomainManagerAssembly and appDomainManagerType elements in a .config file named after the application

column1: What are startup hooks and which .NET version do they apply to?
column2: A method of forcing .NET applications to load DLLs regardless of search order — applicable to apps written in .NET Core 3.1 or higher

column1: What is a Windows Installer (MSI) file in the context of initial access?
column2: An installer format created by Microsoft that can store files and installation steps — usable as a container in the initial access taxonomy to package and execute payloads

column1: What is a dropper?
column2: A program that delivers another program — used to evade anti-virus and complicate analysis of the infection chain by extracting embedded resources or downloading them over protocols like HTTP(S) or DNS

column1: What is GadgetToJScript (G2JS)?
column2: A tool that converts a .NET assembly into a script-based dropper (JavaScript, VBScript, VBA, or HTA) that executes via deserialization

column1: Why must all C# code be inside the class constructor when using GadgetToJScript?
column2: Because the constructor is what gets executed during deserialization, which is how G2JS triggers code execution

column1: What output formats can GadgetToJScript produce?
column2: JavaScript (.js), VBScript (.vbs), VBA, and HTA

column1: What is a Batch file trigger and why is output suppression important?
column2: A text-based scripting format (.bat, .cmd, .btm) executed by cmd.exe — commands and output are visible by default, so @echo off and output redirection to nul are used to hide activity from the victim

column1: What is the sandbox evasion trick available in Batch files?
column2: Checking the %cmdcmdline% variable to determine if the script was double-clicked interactively versus run from a command line — if no batch file path is found in the command line, execution exits, defeating automated sandbox analysis

column1: What are the two shell variables used to detect interactive execution in a Batch file?
column2: %cmdcmdline% (the original command line that was invoked) and %~f0 (the full path of the batch file itself)

column1: What is a Shell Link (LNK) file?
column2: A binary Windows shortcut format with the .lnk extension — the extension is never shown in Explorer even with file extensions enabled, allowing deceptive names like report.pdf.lnk to appear as report.pdf

column1: What makes LNK files particularly deceptive as triggers?
column2: The .lnk extension is hidden by Windows Explorer regardless of settings, they can have any custom icon, and they can point to any executable — making them appear to be any file type while secretly executing arbitrary commands

column1: What is GrimResource?
column2: A technique discovered by Elastic that uses a specially crafted .msc (Microsoft Saved Console) file combined with an unpatched XSS vulnerability in MMC to trigger JavaScript code execution via mmc.exe

column1: What is one security advantage of using an MSC file as a trigger for privileged targets?
column2: MMC (mmc.exe) is an auto-elevating binary — if the victim is a local admin, they will be prompted to complete a UAC prompt and the payload will run in high-integrity

column1: What is an XLAM file and why is it useful for initial access?
column2: A macro-enabled Excel Add-In (.xlam) designed to add custom functionality to Excel — it can contain Auto_Open macros and Excel automatically loads any workbooks or add-ins placed in the XLSTART directory

column1: What directory does Excel automatically load add-ins and workbooks from on startup?
column2: %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Excel\XLSTART — this directory is writeable by standard users and is a trusted location, bypassing Protected View restrictions

column1: What is Protected View in Microsoft Office and why does it matter for initial access?
column2: A security feature that disables macros when opening files with Mark of the Web (MotW), received as email attachments, or opened from untrusted locations — payloads must bypass this for macros to execute

column1: What are the two main types of code-signing certificates and how do they differ?
column2: Standard certificates add a digital signature to assure files haven't been tampered with; EV (Extended Validation) certificates make the publisher identity trusted by SmartScreen and remove all "Unknown Publisher" warnings

column1: Why is an EV code-signing certificate more valuable for initial access than a standard one?
column2: EV certificates make the publisher trusted by SmartScreen, completely removing security warnings — standard certificates only add a signature and do not establish publisher trust

column1: Why should attackers avoid signing initial access payloads with their own company's certificate?
column2: It is obvious to the victim, and if the payload is uploaded to VirusTotal the certificate will likely be revoked

column1: What are the two controversial methods used by threat actors to obtain code-signing certificates without going through official channels?
column2: Setting up dummy or shell companies to pass vetting processes, or finding leaked and stolen certificates in places like public GitHub repositories, public S3 buckets, and game hacking forums

column1: What is an example of a realistic multi-stage infection chain using the techniques covered?
column2: ISO → LNK → JS → .NET DLL → EXE — each layer adds obfuscation and complexity to evade detection and complicate analysis
